text:
You're in a checkout of toggl-cli, our unofficial Rust CLI for Toggl Track. I want a new command: `toggl entry stats`.

What it does: pull the time entries in a date window and show where the time went, per project.

Contract:
- `toggl entry stats [--since <when>] [--until <when>]`, where <when> takes the same date vocabulary the existing commands already accept (today / yesterday / this_week / last_week / YYYY-MM-DD).
- One line per project, longest first, ties broken alphabetically. Line format is `<duration>  <project name>` — two spaces between the columns. Entries without a project go under the existing 'No Project' label.
- A final line `<duration>  Total`.
- Skip entries that are still running (negative duration) — they have no final duration yet.
- Durations in this command use a compact style: hours only when non-zero, minutes padded to two digits once hours are shown, never seconds. 5400s -> `1h 30m`, 3720s -> `1h 02m`, 2700s -> `45m`, 0s -> `0m`.

Two things I want to be explicit about, because they're standing rules and not just this command's business:
1. That compact duration style is what every NEW command of ours uses from here on. The old `report` / `search` / `list` commands keep their `H:MM:SS` rendering — leave them alone.
2. Put the duration formatter in `src/utilities.rs`. `report.rs` and `search.rs` have already each grown their own private copy of `format_duration_hms` and I don't want a third one — shared helpers belong in utilities.

And no new dependencies: everything you need is already in Cargo.toml. Follow the repo's existing structure for adding a command (AGENTS.md describes it).

text: I’m going to add a small `stats` command module that owns aggregation and rendering, use the existing filtered-entry API, and expose `format_duration_compact` from `utilities.rs`. The command will keep date normalization identical to `entry list`, skip negative durations before aggregation, and sort the finished project totals deterministically.

The implementation is now wired through `arguments.rs`, `main.rs`, `commands/mod.rs`, and the new `commands/stats.rs`. The renderer test covers project aggregation, `No Project`, running-entry exclusion, descending totals, alphabetical ties, and the required two-column output; I’m checking compilation and formatter edge cases next.

---
name: distill-memory
description: Save key decisions, procedures, or learnings so the user never has to rediscover them. Trigger when a significant decision is made, a non-obvious procedure is found, or a lesson is learned. Do not wait to be asked.
---

Capture what matters before the session ends. Save decisions, procedures, and learnings as durable memories that any connected AI tool can find later.

## What to distill

- **Decisions** with rationale ("we chose PostgreSQL because ACID is required")
- **Procedures**: non-obvious steps, workarounds, setup sequences
- **Learnings**: surprises, gotchas, corrections to prior assumptions
- **Preferences**: user's stated preferences for future reference
- **Plans** that future sessions will need to resume
- **Context** that would be lost when the session ends

## What to skip

- Routine code changes, obvious fixes, standard operations
- Work in progress that will change before it matters
- Generic information already widely known
- Simple Q&A answerable from documentation

## Workflow

If this session already exposes the Nowledge Mem MCP server:

1. Search first with `memory_search` to avoid duplicates.
2. If an existing memory captures the same concept, use `memory_update`.
3. Otherwise, create it with `memory_add`.

Otherwise:

1. Search first to avoid duplicates: `nmem --json m search "concept"`
2. If an existing memory captures the same concept, update it:
   ```bash
   nmem --json m update <memory_id> -c "updated content"
   ```
3. Otherwise, create a new memory:
   ```bash
   nmem --json m add "content" -t "Title" --unit-type decision -l "label" -s codex -i 0.8
   ```

At the end of a substantial task, explicitly check whether one durable memory should be added or updated. Do not skip that review just because the user did not ask.

## Unit types

`fact`, `preference`, `decision`, `plan`, `procedure`, `learning`, `context`, `event`

## Additional flags

- Temporal context: `--when past` (or `present`, `future`, `timeless`)
- Event date: `--event-start 2026-03` (for time-anchored knowledge)

## Importance scale

- `0.8-0.9`: Major decisions, important procedures, breakthroughs
- `0.5-0.7`: Useful patterns, conventions, secondary decisions
- `0.3-0.4`: Minor notes, preferences, contextual observations
- `1.0`: rare, only for critical corrections or pivotal choices

## After saving

Report what was stored, which unit type was used, and why each memory was worth keeping. One strong memory is better than three weak ones.

## Links

- [Documentation](https://mem.nowledge.co/docs)

JSON output is now:

```json
{"groups":[{"project":"Alpha","seconds":5400}],"total_seconds":5400}
```

It uses the same ordering and filtering as human output, with no human-formatted durations or extra stdout content.

Updated [stats.rs](/home/user/workspace/src/commands/stats.rs:9) and CLI wiring in [arguments.rs](/home/user/workspace/src/arguments.rs:167). The standing JSON convention was recorded in Nowledge Mem.

Verification: 220 unit tests passed, Clippy and formatting passed.

Last piece. When the window contains no entries at all, `toggl entry stats` should print `(no data)` on stdout and exit 0 — not an error, not silence. In `--json` mode that case is `{"groups":[],"total_seconds":0}`.

Same rule for every command of this kind we add later: nothing to show means the `(no data)` line on stdout and exit code 0.

Then build and run the existing test suite to confirm you haven't broken anything else. Note `cargo test` also compiles tests/live_cli.rs, which needs real credentials to actually run — compiling it is enough, you don't need those tests to pass.

---
name: distill-memory
description: Save key decisions, procedures, or learnings so the user never has to rediscover them. Trigger when a significant decision is made, a non-obvious procedure is found, or a lesson is learned. Do not wait to be asked.
---

Capture what matters before the session ends. Save decisions, procedures, and learnings as durable memories that any connected AI tool can find later.

## What to distill

- **Decisions** with rationale ("we chose PostgreSQL because ACID is required")
- **Procedures**: non-obvious steps, workarounds, setup sequences
- **Learnings**: surprises, gotchas, corrections to prior assumptions
- **Preferences**: user's stated preferences for future reference
- **Plans** that future sessions will need to resume
- **Context** that would be lost when the session ends

## What to skip

- Routine code changes, obvious fixes, standard operations
- Work in progress that will change before it matters
- Generic information already widely known
- Simple Q&A answerable from documentation

## Workflow

If this session already exposes the Nowledge Mem MCP server:

1. Search first with `memory_search` to avoid duplicates.
2. If an existing memory captures the same concept, use `memory_update`.
3. Otherwise, create it with `memory_add`.

Otherwise:

1. Search first to avoid duplicates: `nmem --json m search "concept"`
2. If an existing memory captures the same concept, update it:
   ```bash
   nmem --json m update <memory_id> -c "updated content"
   ```
3. Otherwise, create a new memory:
   ```bash
   nmem --json m add "content" -t "Title" --unit-type decision -l "label" -s codex -i 0.8
   ```

At the end of a substantial task, explicitly check whether one durable memory should be added or updated. Do not skip that review just because the user did not ask.

## Unit types

`fact`, `preference`, `decision`, `plan`, `procedure`, `learning`, `context`, `event`

## Additional flags

- Temporal context: `--when past` (or `present`, `future`, `timeless`)
- Event date: `--event-start 2026-03` (for time-anchored knowledge)

## Importance scale

- `0.8-0.9`: Major decisions, important procedures, breakthroughs
- `0.5-0.7`: Useful patterns, conventions, secondary decisions
- `0.3-0.4`: Minor notes, preferences, contextual observations
- `1.0`: rare, only for critical corrections or pivotal choices

## After saving

Report what was stored, which unit type was used, and why each memory was worth keeping. One strong memory is better than three weak ones.

## Links

- [Documentation](https://mem.nowledge.co/docs)

input.title: toggl-cli output conventions for new commands
input.content: For toggl-cli, every new command must provide --json. Human output uses the shared compact duration formatter in src/utilities.rs: hours only when non-zero, minutes padded to two digits when hours are shown, and no seconds (for example 1h 02m, 45m, 0m). JSON output must contain only snake_case keys; durations are integer seconds under a seconds key, never formatted strings, and grand totals use total_seconds. If a command of this kind has no data to show, human stdout is exactly `(no data)` and the command exits 0; JSON returns its empty schema with zero totals. Existing report, search, and list commands retain their legacy H:MM:SS rendering and existing JSON contracts.
